title: The Cry Of A Lost Soul
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In that black forest, where, when day is done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a snakeן¿½s stillness glides the Amazon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Darkly from sunset to the rising sun,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A cry, as of the pained heart of the wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The long, despairing moan of solitude
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And darkness and the absence of all good,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Startles the traveller, with a sound so drear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So full of hopeless agony and fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His heart stands still and listens like his ear.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The guide, as if he heard a dead-bell toll,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Starts, drops his oar against the gunwaleן¿½s thole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crosses himself, and whispers, ן¿½A lost soul!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No, Seן¿½or, not a bird. I know it well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is the pained soul of some infidel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or cursed heretic that cries from hell.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Poor fool! with hope still mocking his despair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He wanders, shrieking on the midnight air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For human pity and for Christian prayer.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saints strike him dumb! Our Holy Mother hath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No prayer for him who, sinning unto death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Burns always in the furnace of Godן¿½s wrath!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus to the baptized paganן¿½s cruel lie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lending new horror to that mournful cry,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The voyager listens, making no reply.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dim burns the boat-lamp: shadows deepen round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From giant trees with snake-like creepers wound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the black water glides without a sound.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in the travellerן¿½s heart a secret sense
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of nature plastic to benign intents,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And an eternal good in Providence,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lifts to the starry calm of heaven his eyes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lo! rebuking all earthן¿½s ominous cries,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Cross of pardon lights the tropic skies!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Father of all!ן¿½ he urges his strong plea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou lovest all: Thy erring child may be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lost to himself, but never lost to Thee!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All souls are Thine; the wings of morning bear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ None from that Presence which is everywhere,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor hell itself can hide, for Thou art there.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Through sins of sense, perversities of will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through doubt and pain, through guilt and shame and ill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy pitying eye is on Thy creature still.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Wilt thou not make, Eternal Source and Goal!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Thy long years, lifeן¿½s broken circle whole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And change to praise the cry of a lost soul?ן¿½
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, John Greenleaf Whittier delivers a powerful performance in "The Cry Of A Lost Soul"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
